metaGlobalVars.seoTitle: Guest to Entra Admin in 9 Steps: Microsoft Entra ID Attack
metaGlobalVars.seoDescription: Discover how attackers can escalate privileges in Entra ID using Azure VMs, PRT theft, and device identity abuse. Learn how the “Evil VM” attack unfolds, and how you can defend against it.

title: Going Beyond Detection: How to Counter Iranian Nation-State TTPs with AI-Powered Insights

fields.heading: Going Beyond Detection: How to Counter Iranian Nation-State TTPs with AI-Powered Insights
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted: This blog explores the importance of using a proactive, prevention-first approach to identity security. Leveraging AI-driven insights to model threats, early warning telemetry to disrupt cyberattacks, and other proactive approaches can help harden your defenses against nation-state tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).

fields.headingSection: Why Iranian TTPs Require a More Proactive Identity Security Approach
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Cyberattacks continue to occur at alarming rates, despite significant investments by organizations in detection capabilities. The reality is current detection methods are often reactive, noisy, and unreliable. They yield low fidelity results, false-positives, and all too often detect threat activity too late in the ATT&CK lifecycle. This is largely due to the growing amount of data these tools need to process to detect threat actors’ activities.

In response, the industry has begun to explore artificial intelligence (AI), hoping it will accelerate detection capabilities, reduce the noise, and improve the accuracy of telemetry data.

Detection telemetry is valuable for understanding threat actor behaviors and provides a wealth of indicators for cyber defenders, but it, too, is typically reactive and collected post-breach. As threats become stealthier and more sophisticated, organizations must adopt a more proactive approach, using early warning telemetry around identity security posture to prevent attacks before they happen.

This blog explores the importance of using a proactive, prevention-first approach to identity security that leverages AI-driven insights to model threats, early warning telemetry to disrupt cyberattacks, and other proactive approaches to help harden your defenses against nation-state tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs).

fields.headingSection: Prevention Is the New Detection
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
In other words, prevention is now just as critical (if not more so) than detection because it disrupts threat actors’ behaviors and activities. Preventative approaches, such as improving identity hygiene and reducing the identity attack surface, increase resiliency against cyberattacks. Most breaches begin with a simple compromised password, which threat actors use as a starting point to gain elevated privileges, move laterally, and establish persistence.

This is why using early warning telemetry around your identity security posture is essential for prevention. It allows organizations to understand and visualize how poor identity hygiene and exposures in the identity attack surface can be exploited by threat actors to launch cyberattacks, enabling them to implement mitigations to protect themselves.

These preventative approaches align perfectly with “defend and protect forward” philosophies adopted by the Department of Defense (DoD) and CISA’s “Shields Up” campaign. These philosophies recognize that cyber engagement is persistent and requires proactive capabilities, like prevention, to disrupt threat actors effectively.

fields.headingSection: Cyber Resiliency Approaches

rows: Hardens networks and improves resilience before attack
Disrupts adversaries before they can act
Reduces attack surface, blocks known vectors, raises adversary costs
table.table:
StrategyFocusRole of Prevention
Protect ForwardCivilian, defensiveHardens networks and improves resilience before attack
Defend ForwardMilitary, offensiveDisrupts adversaries before they can act
PreventionBothReduces attack surface, blocks known vectors, raises adversary costs

fields.headingSection: Identity Insights: Strengthening Your Infrastructure and Environment
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
As attacker dwell time shrinks and exploits accelerate, relying solely on detection is no longer enough to prevent a compromise. Just as the military uses early warning signs to defend and protect forward, formalizing and codifying that approach in daily cyber operations is essential to prevent and disrupt cyberattacks, especially concerning your identity security posture.

This is where BeyondTrust Identity Security Insights® comes in. This AI-powered solution provides early warning telemetry into an organization’s identity security posture, illuminating critical areas such as:

1) Identity Hygiene

Identity Security Insights helps you improve identity hygiene by pinpointing:

  • Misconfigurations in your identity infrastructure
  • Excessive privileges and standing privileges
  • Exposed passwords and secrets
  • Vulnerable human and non-human accounts

2) Identity Attack Surface

Identity Security Insights has the ability to:

  • Map your True Privilege™, showing the actual, effective access an identity (human, machine, or workload) holds. This means visualizing complex interactions across your identity estate from a threat actor’s perspective, illuminating hidden attack paths, and answering “What an identity can ultimately do”.
  • Identify your Paths to Privilege™, detailing how an identity or account can gain elevated access. This involves mapping exploitable pathways in your identity security posture, focusing on connections, entitlements, and configurations, and addressing “How an identity can get to do what it can ultimately do”.

3) Behavior Analytics

Identity Security Insights uses behavior analytics to detect suspicious activity and threats targeting an organization’s identity security posture. This includes identifying:

  • Session hijacking attempts
  • Kerberos and password spray attempts
  • Anomalous activity associated with privileged sessions and accounts

fields.headingSection: Sourced Threat Intelligence for Iranian Nation-State Actors
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted: Recently, Unit 42, the threat intelligence component of Palo Alto Networks, provided a Threat Briefing on state-sponsored Iranian capabilities, mapping Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs) to specific threat groups. This was complemented by a joint statement from CISA, FBI, Department of Defense Cyber Crime Center (DC3), and the National Security Agency (NSA), warning about potential cyber activity from Iranian nation-state actors and their affiliates. Intelligence suggests that critical infrastructure organizations are being targeted and need to remain vigilant. Unit 42’s threat intelligence categorizes the initial access methods used by these threat actors and their operations in the following ways:

fields.description: Figure 1: Behavior analytics mapped to Iranian threat actors’ TTPs, sourced from Unit 42's Threat Research Center, June 25, 2025: https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/iranian-cyberattacks-2025/

fields.body:
Above, we’ve mapped behavior analytics to Unit 42's threat intelligence to allow organizations to see how early warning telemetry can disrupt threat actors' behaviors. This also helps cybersecurity defenders contextualize known threats based on the specific TTPs employed by these actors. Connecting behavior analytics with TTPs in this novel way, as shown in Figure 1, significantly elevates an organization's cyber defense posture and responsiveness to cyberattacks.

To build resilience against identity-based attacks, it’s essential to disrupt the initial access methods commonly used by threat actors. Threat actors are known to strike during periods of geopolitical tension and conflict, primarily focusing on espionage and targeting various sectors, especially critical infrastructure. Unit 42’s threat intelligence observed that these threat actors employ TTPs, such as targeted spear-phishing, password attacks, and the exploitation of known vulnerabilities (CVEs). They also use sophisticated social engineering tactics, often deploying custom malware to gain remote control of infected devices. This backdoor through remote access capabilities allows threat actors to deploy additional payloads and retrieve information about the infected devices, which is then sent to a command and control (C2) server.

fields.headingSection: Leaning in With Identity Security Insights
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted: Staying vigilant and understanding the impact of geopolitics on cybersecurity is important for calibrating your organization’s situational awareness around emerging threats. However, for organizations that heavily rely on indicators of compromise (IoCs), codifying threat intelligence from situational awareness into daily cyber operations can be challenging. By connecting threat intelligence to and across your identity attack surface, Identity Security Insights provides early warning telemetry that cyber defenders can use to prevent imminent cyberattacks. This disruption is needed to shift the balance of power back to cyber defenders.

fields.description: Figure 2:  Snapshot of Identity Security Insights hygiene detections

fields.body:
One way to implement proactive hardening is to lean in with Identity Security Insights. This allows you to map precisely how your identity security posture can be used by threat actors to launch cyberattacks. Identity-related telemetry provides telltale signs of threat actor engagement, and using it to inform cyber operations, such as managing and eliminating identity hygiene issues before threat actors can leverage them (as shown in Figure 2), is essential for preventing and disrupting identity-related attacks.

You cannot protect what you do not know. Given the complexity and rapid changes in IT environments where misconfigurations and shadow IT are prone to happen, illuminating these issues as early warning signs aligns with a “defend and protect forward” mindset. This helps organizations prioritize remediation and mitigation based on threat intelligence from TTPs. Building resistance against these TTPs is a preventive approach that shifts left in the MITRE ATT&CK lifecycle, disrupting the initial access capabilities identified for Iranian nation-state actors and affiliates.

What’s even more powerful is demonstrating how an identity can get to do what it can ultimately do, highlighting the escalation paths within a True Privilege graph, as seen below in Figure 3. This visualization gives cyber defenders context and understanding of how a threat actor could compromise an account or identity. Modeling identity threats is a novel way for organizations to take control of their identity security posture, well before a threat actor shows up.

fields.description: Figure 3: True Privilege graph, powered by Identity Security Insights, revealing exposures and pathways

fields.body:
This graph shows the True Privilege of an identity, with the orange highlighting unintended Paths to Privilege. In this specific example, a user’s Entra ID is assigned to a directory role in Application Admin. The Application Admin directory role allows for the impersonation of a service principal and the abuse of its API permissions, to do things like adding privileged roles to users, or changing passwords, MFA factors, and more.

Using behavior analytics to identify real-time threats to users provides additional context to help cyber defenders pivot to what matters the most. As shown in Figure 1, behavior analytics can be used to build situational awareness around nation-state actors who leverage sophisticated social engineering campaigns, password attacks, and spear-phishing in initial attack vectors. Knowing how to codify this intelligence into daily cyber operations not only builds resiliency against cyberattacks but also helps cyber defenders reduce the blast radius within their identity security posture.

fields.description: Figure 4:  Behavioral detections provided by Identity Security Insights

fields.body:
If initial access methods were successfully used against the account in the True Privilege™ graph, the threat actor could gain unauthorized access to service principals. This would allow them to access resources and perform actions within a cloud environment or enterprise network. This presents a significant security risk because service principals often have broad permissions to automate tasks or integrate applications.

Connecting behavior analytics (as shown in Figure 4) to your overall identity security posture is a powerful way to contextualize identity-related attacks in real-time. These are common attack vectors that cyber defenders must hunt for and be aware of within their operational environment. Ultimately, Identity Security Insights provides organizations with a powerful tool to operationalize proactive threat hunting to find and fix identity-related issues before being exposed by threat actors.

fields.headingSection: Identity Insights: The Context That Matters
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Identity security is the context that cyber defenders need to elevate awareness around cyberattacks. A compromised account and elevated privileges are common attack vectors that threat actors leverage in their attack chains to launch cyberattacks. Being vigilant with continuous monitoring around your identity security posture will help prevent and disrupt these attacks.

BeyondTrust’s Identity Security Insights product provides a wealth of telemetry that drives crucial context about an identity. This includes insights into its associated hygiene, potential threats that can target it, and most importantly, the risk an identity poses to an organization if it is overprivileged or poorly managed. Illuminating these aspects of identity security provides early warning telemetry to help organizations better understand ways in which their identity security posture can be used and abused by threat actors. Contextualizing these threats and risks is foundational for preventing and accelerating the disruption of cyberattacks.

Ready to gain critical insights into your own identity security posture and proactively defend against advanced threats? Take the first step towards elevating your cyber defense with a no-cost Identity Security Risk Assessment today.

metaGlobalVars.seoDescription: Discover how to counter Iranian nation-state cyber threats using AI-powered insights and proactive identity security. Learn how early warning telemetry and prevention-first strategies can stop TTPs before they escalate.

title: 10 Must-Have Certifications for IT Support Professionals

fields.heading: 10 Must-Have Certifications for IT Support Professionals
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted: Whether you're launching, relaunching, or leveling up your IT career, certifications can help you build credibility, stay current with evolving technologies, and demonstrate your value. In this blog, we explore the top IT support certifications that deliver the greatest impact for your organization and your long-term career growth.

fields.headingSection: What is an IT Support or Help Desk Certification?
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
IT support certifications and help desk certifications are recognized acknowledgments of specific experience, knowledge, and skills relating to IT technologies and services. They are often critical to career advancement and can make all the difference for an entry-level help desk professional who is looking to distinguish themselves from the competition.

As organizations implement new technologies, such as AI and cloud computing, 77% of IT decision-makers have reported concern over finding the right talent to meet growing technology needs. Revature’s State of IT Skills Survey also reported upskilling and reskilling as the #1 strategy for closing the IT Skills Gap in 2025. Certifications are a great way to prove your expertise in meeting these evolving use cases and technologies.

Typically, education requirements for a computer support specialist include an associate’s degree, while user support specialists require some college courses. More technical positions may require a degree in a certain field, such as computer and information technology or engineering. However, relevant IT support desk certifications can often act as an alternative, particularly when combined with in-field experience, allowing even those with just a high school diploma to achieve the same or more career success with the relevant information technology (IT) certifications.

It is important to note, however, that the nature of technology requires ITSM professionals to continue to obtain new certifications and recertify to maintain their knowledge and skills through the continuous innovations and changes of the technologies they work with.

Whether you are looking to start or “reboot” your IT career or stay up to date on the skills and knowledge needed to work with the ever-changing technologies, security considerations, and best practices of the profession, it is important to accrue the Information Technology (IT) certs that are most likely to have the biggest impact on your organization—and your career.

This blog discusses the top ten recommended IT support certifications, along with a few insights on why they are so impactful.

fields.headingSection: The Ten Most Recommended IT Support Certifications
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
1. Microsoft Certifications

Microsoft certifications  train and affirm the technical skills required to perform Microsoft technology-related positions within an organization. They are some of the most critical certs for IT support professionals to get simply because of the prevalence of Microsoft in the office. As of 2024, Microsoft Windows is running on 1.6 billion active devices. 

Microsoft’s services, such as Office 365, Azure, and emerging tools like Copilot, are also very popular amongst top businesses. For instance, a 2024 report revealed that almost 70% of the Fortune 500 uses Microsoft 365 Copilot. Becoming well-versed in Microsoft’s suite of products can provide a strong foundation for IT support professionals.

Which Microsoft certifications should you choose?

Microsoft offers more than 250 certifications for different levels of expertise. The best places to start if you have never obtained Microsoft certifications are fundamentals courses such as Microsoft 365 Certified: Fundamentals, among others related to specific products.

In addition, Microsoft offers certifications tailored to support engineers such as:

  • Microsoft Certified: Dynamics 365 Business Central Developer Associate
  • Microsoft Certified: Windows Server Hybrid Administrator Associate

How do you gain Microsoft certifications?

Microsoft certifications are exam-based certifications. Different levels of certifications correspond to levels of expertise, and the certifications may need to be earned in sequential order. Microsoft typically offers certified guidance programs for free, and the typical cost for an exam is around $165 USD. While fundamental certifications never expire, learners must take periodic renewal assessments to retain intermediate or advanced certifications over time.

fields.body: Businesses globally rely on Microsoft, and there is a high demand for professionals who are skilled in Microsoft technologies. The benefit of Microsoft fundamentals certification is that they do not expire. Other Microsoft Certifications can translate into such roles as Developer, Administrator, Solution Architect, and Security Engineer. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

2. Certified Information Systems Security Professional (CISSP)

The next most important cert is the CISSP certification. Granted by the International Information System Security Certification Consortium, CISSP remains one of the most respected and sought-after cybersecurity certifications.

The CISSP provides training in critical areas, including security and risk management, identity and access management, security operations, and software development security. It also validates the knowledge, skills, and experience of senior cybersecurity managers, ITSM, and technical desk professionals. CISSP was one of the original certifications included in the DoD Directive 8570.1m (now updated to the DoDM 8140.03), which was implemented to ensure cybersecurity professionals developed a baseline understanding of fundamental cybersecurity principles and practices. It remains the dominant certification used/obtained for those in numerous cybersecurity positions.

As of 2024, more than 165,000 professionals worldwide hold CISSP designations. Additionally, anyone who successfully obtains this prestigious certification becomes a member of (ISC)², the world’s largest nonprofit association of cybersecurity professionals. (ISC)² offers many added benefits, including access to exclusive professional development courses, industry events, and more.

How do you gain the CISSP certification?

The process for gaining the CISSP certification requires both an exam and an experience component:

  • Complete five years of cumulative, paid work experience. The professional must first gain sufficient experience in cybersecurity and IT security management.
  • Obtain an endorsement. These experience requirements must be verified by others in the industry who hold CISSP.
  • Pass the exam. The professional must complete the CISSP exam, which is about six hours long.

Once the CISSP certification is obtained, the professional must continue to accumulate experience and continuing professional education credits (CPEs) every year.

Certification in CISSP illustrates the required technical and managerial skills needed to effectively design, engineer, and manage the overall security comportment of an organization. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

3. IT Infrastructure Library (ITIL)

The ITIL (IT Infrastructure Library) offers a range of certifications based on difficulty, from entry-level to expert. It is described as “the most widely accepted approach to IT service management (ITSM) in the world.”

Developed by the Cabinet Office, this IT support cert is based on ITIL 4, a widely accepted ITSM framework that has recently been adapted to align with agile/DevOps concepts. It provides students with training on the key concepts of IT and digital services. This includes foundational concepts such as how modern organizations operate, how value streams can improve agility, which behavioral principles lead to stronger results, and other integral service management terms. There are currently more than 2,000,000 ITIL-certified professionals worldwide.

How do you obtain ITIL certifications?

ITIL certifications are obtained through a set of exams, each difference in difficulty administering a different cert. Those who are new to ITIL must start with the ITIL Foundation module, an entry-level certification. There are no prerequisites to taking the exam, but exam administrators recommend taking any of the ITIL training courses available on the website as preparation. The cost of taking the exam can range from around $600 USD to $2,200 USD, based on the study materials used and whether portions can be taken online.

Certification in ITIL (Information Technology Infrastructure Library) provides the basic structure for the standardization of selecting, planning, delivering, maintaining, and managing the overall lifecycle of IT services within an organization. It allows for greater efficiency and predictable service delivery. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

4. Cisco Certified Network Associate (CCNA)

The CCNA training course and exam teach networking fundamentals, IP services, security fundamentals, automation, and programmability. Obtaining the CCNA validates that you have the professional expertise and skills to manage and optimize today's most advanced networks. The certification offers a range of courses covering cyber ops, collaboration, data center, security, and other topics. IT and ITSM professionals say these courses are critical for those utilizing Cisco products.

Once you’ve completed the CCNA certification, you can take a variety of additional certifications with further specialization at the professional and then expert level. These advanced IT support desk certifications typically require completion of a core and at least one concentration exam.

How do you obtain the CCNA certification?

To become CCNA certified, an IT support professional must gain a passing grade on the 200-301 CCNA exam, which tests knowledge and skills relative to network fundamentals, network access, IP connectivity, IP services, security fundamentals, and automation and programmability. There are no formal prerequisites for the exam, but it is recommended that you have one or more years of experience implementing and administering Cisco solutions. The cost of the CCNA exam is around $300 USD.

Cisco CCNA certification offers professionals an in-depth understanding of networking concepts. It fortifies networking specialists' ability to maintain existing skills and knowledge. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

5. CompTIA A+

The CompTIA A+ certification is an entry-level IT certification that validates key skills in hardware, networking, mobile devices, operating systems, troubleshooting, virtualization/cloud computing, security, and operational procedures. It is largely considered to be the starting point for IT professionals, helping to improve their help desk abilities and learn the fundamentals of IT support, including how to install, maintain, and configure PCs, laptops, mobile devices, and printers, and how to leverage remote support tools to do so.

Many ITSM professionals believe CompTIA A+ to be the most impactful cert, pointing to it as providing key foundational knowledge that will become the basis for subsequent training.

How do you obtain the CompTIA A+?

The CompTIA A+ is an exam-based certification. IT professionals are required to pass two exams, Core 1 (220-1101) and Core 2 (220-1102), that review virtualization, hardware, network, and mobile device efficiency. Each of these exams requires a voucher that costs around $260 USD. It is strongly recommended that administrators have a minimum of 9-12 months of experience before working towards this certification.

CompTIA A+ certification is the industry standard for beginning a career in IT and is the preferred qualifying credential for IT operational and technical IT support roles. As the certification is not vendor-specific, it allows the bearer to focus on building their technical foundations. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

6. CompTIA Network+

The CompTIA Network+ certification is an intermediate-level certification that focuses on troubleshooting network issues within IT software, including how to diagnose inconsistent or faulty connections, how to maintain a network, and how to prevent outages and overflow consequences.

How do you obtain the CompTIA Network+?

The CompTIA Network+ certification is an exam-based cert. The exam costs approximately $370 USD. Prerequisites include the CompTIA A+ certification and a recommended 9-12 months of networking experience.

Much like CompTIA A+, CompTIA Network+ is not vendor-specific, but focuses on the knowledge and skills required to design and implement functional networks. In addition to the networking foundation, the holder of the certification is expected to have the skills needed to configure, manage, and maintain network devices, such as switches and routers to segment network traffic and create flexible and adaptable networks. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

Additional CompTIA certifications

While CompTIA A+ is the highest recommended CompTIA certification for entry-level IT support professionals, more advanced or specialized CompTIA certifications may also include:

Entry-level

  • CompTIA ITF+ certification – While the CompTIA A+ certification is designed for entry-level IT professionals, those requiring more foundational knowledge about the IT support profession may also benefit from the CompTIA ITF+ certification. IT Fundamentals (ITF+) is an introduction to foundational technology knowledge and skills, establishing an IT education framework by focusing on essential IT skills and knowledge. The CompTIAF+ certification is obtained by writing the FC0-U61 exam. There are no requisites or experience hours required. The cost of the exam is approximately $140 USD.
  • CompTIA CySA+ certification – CompTIA Cybersecurity Analyst (CySA+) is for cyber professionals who are responsible for threat detection, analysis, and incident response. It validates an IT professional's ability to perform data-driven security assessments, interpret trends, identify vulnerabilities before they can be exploited, and perform other vulnerability management processes. CySA+ places a strong emphasis on the critical cross-funtional communication skills necessary for effective security analysis and compliance.

Intermediate

  • CompTIA Security+ certification – The CompTIA Security+ certification is a global certification validating the baseline skills necessary to perform core security functions. It focuses on preparing candidates to combat emerging threats and take proactive action in preventing the next cyberattack. Security+ is compliant with ISO 17024 standards and is approved by the US DoD to meet directive 8140/8570.01-M requirements. To obtain the CompTIA Security+ certification, IT professionals need to pass the Security+ exam and the prerequisite CompTIA Network+ exam, and they need to have a recommended two years of experience in IT administration with a security focus. The cost of taking the exam is around $400 USD.

Advanced

  • CompTIA Linux+ – The CompTIA Linux+ certification is internationally recognized for validating the baseline competencies that are required for Linux administrators to secure the enterprise, power the cloud and keep systems running. It is the only job-focused Linux certification covering the latest foundational skills demanded by hiring managers. To obtain the CompTIA Linux+ certification, IT support professionals must complete the CompTIA Linux+ exam. Prerequisites include CompTIA A+ and CompTIA Network+ certifications, along with 12 months of Linux admin experience. The cost of the exam is around $370 USD.
  • CompTIA Server+ – The CompTIA Server+ certification is a global certification for ITSM professionals who install, manage, and troubleshoot servers in data centers, as well as on-premises and hybrid environments. It ensures that they have the technical knowledge and skills required to utilize remote support tools to support and secure server hardware and software. It is the only certification that supports administrator-level work in any environment, as it is not restricted to a single platform. To obtain the CompTIA Server+ certification, IT support professionals must complete the CompTIA Server+ exam. Prerequisites include CompTIA A+ certification and a recommended 18 to 24 months of IT experience. The cost of the exam is around $370 USD.
  • CompTIA SecurityX (formerly CompTIA Advanced Security Practitioner, or CASP+) – An advanced cybersecurity certification for security architects and senior security engineers with at least 10 years of experience who are responsible for leading and improving an enterprise’s cybersecurity readiness. SecurityX demonstrates a security practitioner’s proficiency in designing, building, and implementing secure solutions across complex environments while addressing governance, risk, and compliance needs.

7. Microsoft Azure Fundamentals certification

Cloud computing is currently one of the fastest-growing and most in-demand technologies, with Amazon Web Service (AWS), Google Cloud Platform (GCP), IBM Cloud, and Microsoft Azure leading the market. This makes  Azure Certification a considerable commodity for IT and remote support professionals who want to advance or stay relevant in their field. There are several Microsoft Azure role-based certifications available, but Microsoft Azure Fundamentals is the best starting point for launching your certification journey. The Microsoft Azure Fundamentals certification validates an IT support professional’s basic knowledge of cloud services and cloud computing concepts, models, and services.

How do you obtain Microsoft Azure Fundamentals certification?

The Microsoft Azure Fundamentals certification is an exam-based certification requiring completion of the AZ-900 exam. There are no prerequisites for the exam, but the Azure training resources and practice exams are strongly recommended to prepare for the exam. The exam costs around $100 USD.

Other Popular Azure certifications

Following the Microsoft Azure Fundamentals certification, popular additional certifications include:

  • Microsoft Azure Data Fundamentals
  • Microsoft Azure AI Fundamentals
  • Role-based options such as certifications for the Azure Administrator Associate, Data Engineer Associate, and more.

Microsoft Azure is a single cloud service provider that offers role-based certifications that are specifically designed according to market requirements and job roles. Certifications can help secure roles, such as cloud administrator, developer, security engineer, AI engineer, data engineer, solutions architect, and DevOps engineer. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

8. AWS Certification

AWS certifications have become more popular as organizations around the world enter a more cloud-centric work environment. AWS certifications are important because they help validate an ITSM or their team’s technical skills, but they also give certified individuals the knowledge and skill to drive the future of their organization and bring in more clients.

How do you get AWS certified?

There are currently twelve available AWS certs, ranging from foundational- to professional-level. The best certification to start with is the AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner certification, followed by an associate-level or specialty certification exam (the  AWS Solutions Architect – Associate certification is the most popular).

The Top AWS certifications include:

  • AWS Certified AI Practitioner – Foundational
  • AWS Certified SysOps Administrator – Associate
  • AWS Certified Developer – Associate
  • AWS Certified Data Engineer – Associate
  • AWS Certified Machine Learning Engineer – Associate
  • Advanced Networking – Specialty
  • Machine Learning – Specialty
  • Security – Specialty

After gaining the recommended two years of prior AWS Cloud experience, professionals can also obtain advanced Professional-level certifications.

It is strongly recommended that technical IT support professionals take advantage of both the free AWS tier and the many exam preparation courses and practice questions that are available before taking the exam.
fields.linkText: Learn how to secure AWS Root vs IAM Users

fields.body: Like Azure, AWS is a single cloud service provider. As organizations globally continue to increase their investment in cloud technologies, it is vitally important that IT professionals keep pace with the shifting corporate needs. AWS Certification confirms the holder's knowledge and expertise with the tools and technologies needed for this platform. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

9. VMware certification

VMware Certifications test and validate the critical skills required to integrate and manage the vSphere technology driving the infrastructure of many organizations. VMware offers multiple certification levels, ranging from associate to design expert, and certifications are organized into multiple technology areas with levels for those new to the industry as well as experts in the field.

How do you get VMware Certified?

VMware certification is another exam-based certification. Once you have decided on the certification and certification track, simply register for the desired exam.

Focus areas for VMware certifications include:

  • Data Center Virtualization
  • Network Virtualization
  • Cloud Management and Automation
  • End-User Computing
  • Security
  • Application Modernization

VMware certification validates the user's understanding of VMware's security solutions and their ability to provide entry-level support for the security features of VMware products, including NSX-T Data Center, Workspace ONE, and VMware Carbon Black Cloud. This certifies the bearer’s ability to configure, maintain, and troubleshoot those that use vSphere software. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

10. Google IT Support Professional Certificate

The Google IT Support Certificate is a good place to start for individuals who are looking to begin a career in technical IT support or an IT support help desk. The certification course provides the skills needed for an introductory-level job in IT support. There are no prerequisite qualifications or experience hours. The training program focuses on troubleshooting and customer care, networking, operating systems, system administration, security, and AI usage for boosting productivity.

How do you obtain your Google IT Support Professional certification?

To get Google IT Support certified, the online training program and exams must be completed. Costs of the program and exam may vary. There are no prerequisites for obtaining this IT support certification.

Other Key Google Certifications Include:

  • Google Cybersecurity Certificate – Prepares individuals for a career as a cybersecurity analyst by providing training in the identification of common risks, threats, vulnerabilities and mitigation techniques. It also helps prepare individuals for the CompTIA Security+ exam and offers a dual credential when combined with the certification.

Google IT Support Professional Certification certifies holders can perform day-to-day IT support tasks, including computer assembly, wireless networking, installing programs, and customer service. Google IT Support Professional Certification allows for entry-level skills without the added cost and time of acquiring a collegiate degree. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

fields.headingSection: Additional Certifications for IT Support Professionals
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
A few additional IT support certifications for professionals hoping to build or further their careers include:

  • Certified Ethical Hacker (CEH) – Covers ethical hacking techniques (penetration testing, attack vectors, and countermeasures), helping IT security professionals think like a hacker to proactively identify and mitigate vulnerabilities.
  • Certified Information Security Manager (CISM) – Geared towards professionals managing information security programs and teams, CISM validates an individual’s ability to design and oversee an enterprise-wide information security programs, with a focus on information risk management and governance.
  • GIAC Security Essentials (GSEC) – A foundational-to-intermediate certification from SANS, GSEC certifies IT practitioners for demonstrating a broad, practical understanding of information security principles and practices, including access control, cryptography, and incident response.
  • Offensive Security Certified Professional (OSCP) – A hands-on certification requiring candidates to successfully execute real-world penetration testing and ethical hacking in a timed lab environment. OSCP is highly respected for its validation of offensive security skills and persistence.
  • Certified Cloud Security Professional (CCSP) – Jointly offered by (ISC)² and the Cloud Security Alliance (CSA), CCSP validates a candidate’s advanced technical expertise in cloud security architecture, design, operations, and service orchestration.
  • GIAC Certified Incident Handler (GCIH) – Certifies a candidate’s ability to detect, respond to, and resolve computer security incidents. It also emphasizes threat intelligence and hands-on handling of malware, exploits, and intrusions.

fields.headingSection: Why Should I Get an IT Support Certification?
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
There are several reasons to get technical IT support certifications. The main benefits of include:

  • Meeting the growing need for qualified professionals who have proven expertise in emerging technologies
  • Proving that you have the skills needed to succeed in a prospective role
  • Opening up opportunities to grow in your role
  • Proving your commitment to continuous improvement and learning about new and evolving technology

A recent CompTIA report found that 79% of HR professionals in medium-sized and large organizations look for industry-recognized technical certifications during the hiring process. Further, once in the  IT support help desk profession, these certifications can also help with career advancement. According to  the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, the median salary for ‘computer support specialists’ in 2024 was $61,550 per year, or $29.59 per hour.

If you are looking to get into the IT support profession as an ITSM or a technical IT support specialist, IT support certifications, such as the Google IT Support Professional certification, can offer an alternative to the standard college degree. If you have a college degree already, IT support certifications can offer you a professional edge over your competition.

fields.body: Credited certification in each and all of the above items ensures that the bearer can install, secure, operate, and troubleshoot issues within enterprise networks. -- Mike Hewens, Senior Manager, Technical Support, BeyondTrust

title: When Clickbait Goes Bad – How to Protect your Identity & Business from Clickbait Phishing Scams

fields.heading: When Clickbait Goes Bad – How to Protect your Identity & Business from Clickbait Phishing Scams
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted: Clickbait phishing scams exploit emotional triggers like curiosity or fear to trick users into clicking malicious links. This blog, refreshed with updated cybersecurity hygiene strategies, details practical defenses, from link-spotting techniques and reporting protocols to limiting admin privileges and ensuring timely patching. Learn how these expert-recommended steps can significantly reduce the risk of an accidental breach.

fields.headingSection: The Rising Risk of Clickbait Phishing and Why Cyber Hygiene Matters
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Everyone makes mistakes, but when something as simple as a single click can have perilous impacts on your identity, bank account, and business, the stakes get a lot higher. One simple click on a malicious link in an email, text message, or on a website or application (and, in fairness, a double click on a malicious attachment) can mean weeks, months, or even years of recovery—if recovery is even possible. If you make the same mistake on a corporate network, the ramifications can be near business-ending, cost tens of thousands of dollars to mitigate, or make the headlines for yet another security breach or ransomware attack.

If one malicious clickbait scam may destroy you and your business, then why are so many people still making this mistake? This blog discusses the challenges with malicious links and attachments, the best ways to recognize them when they do appear, the emerging risk of AI-driven clickbait creation, and how the impact of phishing can be significantly reduced, if not altogether mitigated, by following simple cybersecurity hygiene guidelines.

fields.headingSection: The Dangers of Clickbait Phishing: Sabotage, Theft & Business Risk
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Whatever you want to call them—scammers, cybercriminals, hackers, attackers, or threat actors (and technically there is a difference)—their goals are fairly similar. They want to find the easiest path to compromise your identity or penetrate your company’s network. Malicious links are the keys to the kingdom for them. With them, they are most likely to do one of two things:

  1. Sabotage: spread malware infections, etc. to disrupt or corrupt data, causing harm or inconvenience to you or your company based on some nefarious mission or nation-state cyber war tactic. Today, it's rare to see malicious links executed for “fun”, such as the exploits in the late 1990s based on bragging rights, proof of concepts, or publicity.
  2. Theft: lure an unsuspecting user to expose valuables, such as data, identities, or money, or to grant an access point to restricted systems or through which they can move laterally to gain access to restricted systems and ultimately exfiltrate data of value and relevant to their mission.

fields.headingSection: How Clickbait Scams Leverage Social Engineering Tactics
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
As we can see, it is highly advantageous for threat actors when they convince you to click seemingly innocuous clickbait links. Clickbait scams fall under the broader category of social engineering attacks. Social engineering leverages an understanding of the way people think and act to manipulate a user’s behavior. All the threat actor needs to do is understand what drives a user’s actions. With that knowledge, they can use deceptive tactics, like making them believe the email or website is real, the source is trustworthy, and that it is urgent to perform an action.

They may also try to insight a heightened emotional response from you (fear, anger, excitement, curiosity, guilt, sadness) by emphasizing trends, controversial news, or other hot topics. Humans are far more likely to take irrational or risky actions when they are in an undesirable or compromised emotional state.

fields.headingSection: If someone in your organization clicked on a malicious link, how far could an attacker escalate within your identity estate?
fields.label: Find out with our free Identity Security Risk Assessment.

fields.headingSection: How to Spot Clickbait and Avoid Phishing Links
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Spotting malicious clickbait can be tricky. Here are five tips to help you navigate whether or not a link delivered via email, over a text, on a website, etc. is malicious:

1. Watch for mistakes.

Mistakes and odd display, construction, or formatting of content, including misspellings, typos, poor grammar, or suspicious link or file names, can indicate a deceptive phishing scheme. The context of the message or the time an email or text in question was sent can also help to tip you off, including malformed times and dates. Likewise, irregularities in the URL, poor image quality, and outdated logos can all indicate malicious intent.

2. Inspect where the link is from.

If you receive a suspicious message—especially anything asking you to click a link or download a document—check to see if it is coming from a legitimate email address, or review your company’s global address list or the social media profile that sent the message. Email addresses that are very clearly incorrect, or that would not be associated with a business account, are a clear giveaway. Additionally, watch for detailed imitators such as substituting numbers or symbols for letters. For example, using 0 for O or + for t can fool most individuals who don’t look close enough.

3. Prove or validate the identity of the sender.

If you receive a suspicious email or message from a seemingly legitimate source, try contacting the source through an alternative communication vehicle, like a phone call. For instance, if you get a message supposedly from your bank, call your bank to inquire about the message. If you get a notification about an online account, avoid the email and log into your account through your usual, verified process to determine whether it’s a legitimate notification or a scam attempt. Don’t click on the link in the email; navigate to the website on your own.

In addition, never use the phone number provided in the suspicious email or sourced from an internet search. Go directly to the company’s website to find contact information. Scammers have been known to flood internet search engines with fake phone numbers for airlines and banks to perpetuate their scams.

4. Pay attention to your emotions.

If the message triggers a curious, fearful, or negative response, consider your elevated emotional response a red flag. Clickbait can be just as threatening as being accosted, and keeping calm is crucial to determining a scam versus reality. In addition, just like drunk driving, avoid answering emails, or clicking on links when impaired. You should always use your best judgment when reviewing any questionable content.

5. Be wary of volunteering information.

Providing even basic data, like your email address or phone number, could leave you vulnerable. If it sounds too good to be true, it probably is. Giveaways and incentives are strong motivators, and thus common targeting methods and attack drivers. As a rule of thumb, never disclose your personally identifiable information to unknown or untrusted sources—especially over the phone or Internet. For example, if your home alarm system asks for the password to access your account, then you are being set up for a scam. No one should ever ask you for your password to access your account. Instead, security questions and PINs should be used to validate your identity.

fields.headingSection: Cybersecurity Hygiene: Your Best Defense Against Clickbait Phishing
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Knowing how to identify and avoid (or better yet, report) a malicious link is an important preventative strategy. But occasionally, we all make mistakes. Whether the email or website looks real or fails to trigger our “fight or flight” response, we are never 100% secure in our ability to spot a bad link. Good cybersecurity hygiene can safeguard you when preventative education and identification fails.

Cyber hygiene, or cybersecurity hygiene, is a set of practices organizations and individuals perform regularly to maintain the health and security of users, devices, networks, clouds, and data. It isn’t unlike our own personal hygiene. Showering frequently and brushing (and flossing) our teeth are the precautionary measures we take to prevent the spread of disease, reduce the risk of cavities, and overall maintain our physical health. In the same way, organizations can follow basic cybersecurity actions regularly to prevent data breaches and other security incidents.

Good cyber hygiene reduces significant risks that come with security incidents such as data compromise and loss, operational interruptions and downtime, financial loss and government fines, damage to reputation, legal liability risks, and more. Furthermore, simple cybersecurity concepts such as least privilege can significantly reduce the impact of a malicious click by limiting the privileges allowed to the associated application or malware.

In other words, when something does go wrong, your cybersecurity hygiene makes the difference between the link you clicked doing nothing at all, or the beginning of a devastating compromise of you, your computer, and your company. This is because basic tenants, like the removal of administrative rights, can stop the injection and execution of malware since the malicious code does not have enough privileges to execute in the first place.

fields.headingSection: 4 Cyber Hygiene Best Practices to Prevent Clickbait Attacks
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
Here are some basic cybersecurity hygiene practices you can implement now to keep you safe even after you have clicked a malicious link:

  1. Update your system and applications regularly. Either allow your operating system and apps to apply recommended security updates, or ensure your organization is applying them in a timely manner after public disclosure. This will help prevent exploitation of any known vulnerabilities associated with clicking an errant link or opening a malicious file.
  2. Use an anti-virus solution and keep it up to date. Ensure your anti-virus solution is properly licensed, receiving updates, and periodically scanning your system for dormant malware or advanced persistent threats.
  3. Interact with your computer as a standard user and not an administrator for daily activities. This concept of least privilege will thwart malware that needs administrative privileges to infect your computer. This one method is by far the most effective at stopping an attack and is recommended by cyber insurance carriers and regulatory compliance bodies alike.
  4. Ditch your old computer. If your operating system is end of life, like Windows 7 or Windows 10 (as of October 2025), consider updating or replacing your system. An end-of-life system no longer receives security patches, and odds are the anti-virus vendor is no longer providing updates since it has been depreciated. It is just not a safe device to have on the Internet, and attackers know this. Vulnerabilities and exploits are easy targets since end users have no way of mitigating the risks. If you cannot afford to replace the hardware, and it is not compatible with a newer operating system, consider initiatives like Google Chrome OS Flex to modernize your operating system and stay protected.

Many other basic cybersecurity hygiene concepts are important to minimize risks like phishing schemes. They can include vulnerability and patch management and endpoint security solutions such as endpoint detection and response (EDR) and endpoint privilege management (EPM).
fields.label: Click here to access the cybersecurity insurance checklist

fields.headingSection: Real-World Scenario: How Good Cyber Hygiene Minimizes the Damage of Clickbait Phishing
fields.bodyRedactorRestricted:
In an ideal scenario, when you identify a phishing email or visit a fraudulent website, based on your training, you will be able to recognize the potential threat. You’ll then close the web page or email and report the threat to your information security department for analysis and to prevent others from receiving the same content, and if needed, have your asset assessed for additional threats. As more devious tactics come into play, however, it can become more and more difficult to spot malicious intent in something as simple as a hyperlink. This is why good cybersecurity hygiene is so important.

Let’s walk through a malicious link and click scenario so we can see where good cybersecurity hygiene comes into play, and the impact it can have on the situation.

The scenario: You open a phishing email or visit a fraudulent website and click on a malicious link. The results could compromise you and your entire organization. What does this look like from a hygiene perspective?

rows: The link attempts to infect your computer with malware when it is clicked and is able to execute based on missing security patches and local administrative privileges.

The link attempts to infect your computer with malware and the security controls in place flag [abnormal system behavior](https://www.beyondtrust.com/solutions/itdr) in real-time. Your information security department is immediately notified of unauthorized privileged access and attempted inappropriate behavior.
The malware exploits the unpatched vulnerabilities in your operating system, browser, or associated third party application and infects your system with a virus.

Your system is regularly patched, and all vulnerabilities are remediated. As a result, the malware cannot continue to exploit your system or others on the network because the vulnerability has been remediated.
Your anti-virus software is outdated and fails to detect a particular virus, allowing it to execute on your system.

Your anti-virus is always up to date, which allows it to detect and eradicate the virus before it can execute. In addition, your anti-virus solution ideally contains [EDR capabilities](https://www.beyondtrust.com/resources/whitepapers/edr-and-epm), so any additional threats from the malware can be identified promptly even if the virus tries to obfuscate its signature.
You have full admin rights on your system, which means you can execute anything on your computer, change critical settings, and even uninstall security applications designed to protect your system.

You’ve removed administrative rights and are interacting with standard user privileges. This means the system has safeguarded the account that does have administrative privileges and has thereby mitigated [75% of your critical Microsoft vulnerabilities](https://www.beyondtrust.com/resources/whitepapers/microsoft-vulnerability-report). This prevents most exploits from executing on your computer, therefore blocking the attack.

Your computer can openly share files, printers, and videos, with other systems on your network. Lateral movement between systems is wide open and ransomware or other vulnerabilities can be leveraged in an attack.

Lateral movement between all assets on a network are restricted and any peer-to-peer communications have been disabled. This prevents the malware and ransomware from spreading unattended from system to system.

A user can launch a remote access session (RDP, SSH, VNC, etc.) to a system anywhere with just a username and password. These open ports can be used during an attack and malware can be injected once a session is active.

Your remote access sessions all use multi-factor authentication (MFA) and follow the concepts of least privilege. The assets accepting remote access do not have any public listening ports to initiate the session or across internal segmented networks. Therefore, these ports cannot be used during an attack and [malware](https://www.beyondtrust.com/resources/glossary/malware) cannot be injected into a session.
